symptoms.symptom_name: Sudden severe unremitting calf and foot pain, not relieved by rest or analgesia

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Sudden rest pain unrelieved by analgesia is the first P of acute arterial occlusion.
Pain out of proportion is a hallmark of compartment syndrome complicating ischaemia.
Cellulitic pain is usually accompanied by heat, erythema and fever and comes on over days, not hyperacutely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pallor and mottling of the foot and lower leg with a mid-calf demarcation line

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pallor with a demarcation line indicates the level of arterial occlusion.
DVT produces a warm, swollen, erythematous limb — the opposite of pale and cold.

symptoms.symptom_name: Perishing coldness of the foot/lower leg to mid-calf

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Perishing cold (poikilothermia) is one of the six Ps of arterial occlusion.
A cold limb argues against the warm, congested limb of venous thrombosis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sensory loss progressing to complete anaesthesia of the foot

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Sensory deficit signifies an immediately threatened (Rutherford IIb) limb — a time-critical escalation trigger.

symptoms.symptom_name: Motor deficit — inability to move the toes / foot drop

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Motor deficit added to sensory loss defines Rutherford IIb — the strongest history-based marker of an emergency limb.
Radiculopathy can cause foot drop but never a pulseless cold limb; here the vascular signs dominate.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absent pedal pulses on palpation (femoral weak; popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial absent)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pulselessness with a preserved but weak proximal (femoral) pulse localises an occlusion at or below the femoral/popliteal level. Clinician-only palpation — sealed answer key; the AI Doctor infers occlusion from the caregiver's cold/pale/absent-Doppler description, not from a palpated exam.

symptoms.symptom_name: Inaudible foot pulse on hand-held Doppler (staff-obtained)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An absent Doppler signal corroborates critical arterial occlusion; staff-obtained and reportable over telehealth, but unverified and not a substitute for formal assessment.
Tense, tender compartments with pain out of proportion indicate impending myonecrosis — clinician palpation, sealed.
Advanced ischaemia with muscle tenderness marks the later, less salvageable stage.

symptoms.symptom_name: CT angiography showing abrupt cut-off of the superficial femoral / popliteal artery

investigation_details.investigation_name: CT angiography of the lower limb
investigation_details.expected_result: Abrupt contrast cut-off in the superficial femoral or popliteal artery with absent distal run-off
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Level of occlusion and revascularisation plan cannot be determined — but imaging happens in hospital and must not delay emergency transfer.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Angiographic arterial cut-off confirms the occlusion and its level — a hospital investigation, never available in real time via telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Known peripheral arterial disease and intermittent claudication

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pre-existing arterial stenosis is the substrate for acute in-situ thrombosis.

symptoms.symptom_name: No erythema, warmth, swelling or fever

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of the inflammatory signs of infection effectively excludes cellulitis.
Absence of warmth and swelling argues against venous thrombosis.

symptom_clusters.cluster_name: The six Ps of acute limb ischaemia
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Pain, pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesia, paralysis and perishing cold together are a near-pathognomonic emergency constellation; the presence of paraesthesia and paralysis marks a threatened, time-critical limb.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The definitive step is emergency transfer for vascular review and revascularisation; the AI Doctor's expected action is to escalate, not to arrange outpatient tests.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: CT angiography of the lower limb
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Localises the arterial occlusion to plan thrombolysis/thrombectomy/bypass — performed in hospital, must not delay transport.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Outpatient lower-limb venous duplex ultrasound for suspected DVT
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Anchoring on DVT and arranging an outpatient scan would be a dangerous under-triage — the limb is pale, cold and pulseless (arterial), and the situation is an emergency.
